policy_statement: Protect quantity and quality of source water for municipal supply and environmental flows through land protection, partnerships, green infrastructure, and recharge improvements.
mechanism_description: Protecting watershed lands and expanding green infrastructure increases groundwater recharge and safeguards source-water quality, improving the resilience of municipal and environmental water supplies.
primary_causal_pathway: Climate hazard (drought/heat/extreme rainfall) → watershed function + green infrastructure → ↑ infiltration/recharge + ↓ contamination/runoff impacts → ↓ water-supply disruption risk
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change: The policy protects and manages source-water watersheds (e.g., land protection/partnerships) and expands green infrastructure/recharge improvements (e.g., permeable surfaces, bioswales, riparian buffers, recharge features).  
(2) Mediating process: Increased infiltration and soil/wetland water retention improves groundwater recharge and baseflows; vegetated/natural cover filters pollutants and reduces erosion/sediment loads, buffering water quality impacts from storms and wildfire runoff.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Greater reliability and quality of municipal/environmental water supplies under climate stress (drought, hotter conditions, more intense precipitation), reducing service interruptions and treatment burdens tied to climate extremes.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced drought/heat-driven water-supply risk by increasing infiltration/groundwater recharge and maintaining cleaner, more reliable source water.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: The stated causal chain is watershed protection + green infrastructure → recharge/quality protection → resilient water supply, which is an impact-reduction (risk management) pathway.  
2) Why Adaptation over alternatives: Although land protection can store carbon (NBS/mitigation) and green infrastructure can marginally reduce energy for pumping/treatment (resource efficiency), the mechanism’s defining and largest climate effect is maintaining water service under climate hazards (drought/variability/extremes).  
3) Secondary categories: Not assigned because mitigation/NBS benefits are not unavoidable for every instance of “green infrastructure and watershed protection” aimed at water resilience (e.g., some projects may be small, non-carbon-significant, or primarily hydraulic without net sink gains).  
4) Edge cases applied: If a policy is explicitly framed as protecting carbon pools/forest sinks as the main goal with water resilience as a co-benefit, it could shift to Nature-Based Solutions; here, the mechanism label and description center on water-supply resilience.  
5) Confirm labels apply to ALL instances: Any implementation of this mechanism (watershed protection + GI for recharge/quality) inherently functions to reduce water-supply vulnerability to climate-driven drought/extremes, fitting Adaptation consistently.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If the same actions are implemented primarily to increase ecosystem carbon sequestration (with water resilience secondary), the primary category could become Nature-Based Solutions; similarly, GI projects focused mainly on stormwater compliance rather than supply reliability may emphasize flood management rather than water-supply resilience (still Adaptation, but different hazard focus).
key_indicators: “Protect water sources”; “Protect quantity and quality of source water for municipal supply”; “environmental flows”; “Improve groundwater recharge”; “expanding green infrastructure”; “land protection, partnerships… recharge improvements”
co_benefits: Improved ecosystem health/habitat and general water-quality benefits (reduced sediment/pollutants) beyond climate-risk reduction.
instance_edge_case_notes: Instrument type could shift to “infrastructure-investment” or “regulatory-mandate” if the full policy text includes committed capital projects (e.g., funded recharge/green streets) or binding green-infrastructure/source-water protection requirements; the excerpt is framed at strategy level (“How we’ll get there”).
